title: Gulf Coast Express expansion begins commissioning | Wood Mackenzie
gist: The Gulf Coast Express pipeline expansion began commissioning in June 2026, adding 570 mmcfd and lifting Waha hub prices from deeply negative territory into positive for the first time since February — but the Permian Basin's structural takeaway deficit requires a further ~4 bcfd of pipeline additions before year-end to achieve price normalisation.

themes.theme: Permian Gas Takeaway Capacity and Waha Pricing

insights.claim: The Gulf Coast Express pipeline expansion began commissioning on 9 June 2026, when two new nomination points appeared near Agua Dulce in South Texas, with initial nominations of 50,000 MMBtu/d into TGP followed by 20,000 MMBtu/d into NGPL on 10 June.
insights.speaker: Daniel  Myers
insights.so_what: Commissioning confirmation via observable nomination data is a reliable, market-verifiable signal — traders and producers can now price in the incremental takeaway relief rather than relying on announced in-service dates.
insights.quote: On 9 June 2026, two new nomination points linked to the Gulf Coast Express (GCX) expansion appeared in South Texas near Agua Dulce.

insights.claim: The Gulf Coast Express expansion adds 570 mmcfd of incremental capacity, increasing total GCX system capacity to approximately 2.57 bcfd from Waha to Agua Dulce.
insights.speaker: Daniel  Myers
insights.so_what: 570 mmcfd of new takeaway capacity is meaningful but modest relative to Permian production of ~24 bcfd — it narrows the gap without closing it, keeping basis differentials structurally weak until further pipelines arrive.
insights.quote: The expansion adds 570 mmcfd of incremental capacity, increasing total GCX system capacity to approximately 2.57 bcfd from Waha to Agua Dulce.

insights.claim: Waha cash prices moved from -$1.03/MMBtu on 8 June to -$0.34/MMBtu on 9 June (commissioning day), and reached positive territory at $0.315/MMBtu for flow-date 16 June — the first positive Waha price since February 2026.
insights.speaker: Daniel  Myers
insights.so_what: The rapid price response confirms that even partial commissioning flows can shift Waha from negative to positive — producers with Waha-linked gas sales see an immediate realisation improvement, and midstream operators with GCX capacity have increased utilisation economics.
insights.quote: Waha prices moved from -$1.03/MMBtu on 8 June to -$0.34/MMBtu on 9 June, reaching positive territory by 16 June for the first time since February.

insights.claim: Waha Hub prices experienced more than 130 consecutive days of negative pricing in 2026, hitting a record low of -$9.52/MMBtu on 16 April.
insights.speaker: Daniel  Myers
insights.so_what: A record negative of -$9.52/MMBtu reflects extreme takeaway congestion that destroyed producer economics for unhedged Permian gas — the severity of the dislocation validates the urgency and value of new pipeline capacity.
insights.quote: This follows a prolonged period of weakness, with more than 130 consecutive days of negative pricing in 2026 and a record low of -$9.52/MMBtu on 16 April.

themes.theme: Structural Permian Gas Imbalance and Infrastructure Pipeline

insights.claim: Permian Basin gas production is currently around 24 bcfd and is expected to exceed 29 bcfd by 2030, meaning the structural takeaway deficit will persist even as new pipeline capacity comes online.
insights.speaker: Daniel  Myers
insights.so_what: A 5+ bcfd production ramp from current levels means Waha basis differentials are unlikely to normalise durably until both the Blackcomb and Hugh Brinson pipelines are operational — investors in Permian-exposed producers remain exposed to prolonged negative basis.
insights.quote: Permian production is currently around 24 Bcf/d and is expected to exceed 29 bcfd by 2030.

insights.claim: Takeaway capacity additions are forecast to outpace Permian production growth by 2027, but the GCX expansion represents an early step toward closing the gap rather than a full solution.
insights.speaker: Daniel  Myers
insights.so_what: The 2027 crossover date is the key investment trigger — producers and midstream operators should model for continued basis weakness through 2026 and into 2027 before expecting sustained price normalisation.
insights.quote: While capacity additions are forecast to outpace production growth by 2027, the GCX expansion represents an early step toward closing the takeaway gap rather than a full solution.

insights.claim: The Blackcomb Pipeline and Hugh Brinson Phase 1 are the next wave of Permian takeaway projects, expected to add around 4 bcfd of additional capacity before year-end 2026.
insights.speaker: Daniel  Myers
insights.so_what: 4 bcfd of incremental year-end capacity additions would significantly reduce Waha basis weakness — project execution risk on these pipelines is the critical variable for unhedged Permian gas producers and for midstream infrastructure investors.
insights.quote: The next wave of projects—including Blackcomb Pipeline and Hugh Brinson Phase 1—is expected to add around 4 bcfd of additional takeaway capacity before year-end.

insights.claim: Until Blackcomb and Hugh Brinson are operational, Waha pricing dynamics are likely to remain sensitive to incremental capacity additions and commissioning progress rather than reflecting a fully rebalanced market.
insights.speaker: Daniel  Myers
insights.so_what: Waha-linked gas pricing will remain event-driven and volatile through year-end — producers should hedge forward and traders should monitor commissioning milestones as the primary price catalyst.
insights.quote: Until then, pricing dynamics are likely to remain sensitive to incremental capacity additions and commissioning progress rather than reflecting a fully rebalanced market.
